county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Cidra, established 1809, carved from Cayey. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Cidra was carved from Cayey in 1809 in the eastern interior highlands. Known as 'La Ciudad de la Eterna Primavera' for its pleasant climate. Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Carmen holds records from the early 19th century. Pre-1809 records are in Cayey's parish archives. Tobacco and coffee farming dominated — hacienda records and notarial protocols at AGPR document landholding families. The Lago de Cidra (reservoir) area displaced some communities in the 20th century — check relocation records. Civil registry from 1885 provides vital records. Cidra's proximity to both Caguas and Cayey means some families appear in records of multiple jurisdictions.

source_profiles.source_name: Cidra Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Cidra municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1809.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Cidra Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Cidra / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Cidra including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Cidra Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Cidra district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1809. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Cidra Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Cidra. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Cidra Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Cidra district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Cidra district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Cidra Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Cidra / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Cidra. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Coamo, established 1579, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Coamo, founded in 1579, is one of Puerto Rico's four oldest settlements (with San Juan, San Germán, and Arecibo). The thermal springs (Baños de Coamo) attracted visitors for centuries. Parroquia San Blas has some of the island's oldest parish registers, with records from the 1600s. As parent to Aibonito (1824), Juana Díaz (1798), Santa Isabel (1842), Salinas (1851), and co-parent to Barranquitas and Villalba, Coamo records are a gateway to a vast interior region. Enslaved persons records are extensive due to sugar and cattle economy. Check Spanish colonial censuses (padrones) at AGPR — Coamo's early padrones are among the most complete.

source_profiles.source_name: Coamo Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Coamo including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane María (2017) caused damage to municipal infrastructure in Comerío. Some local government records and documents may have been affected by flooding and wind damage.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Comerío, established 1826, carved from Bayamón, Barranquitas. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Comerío was carved from Bayamón and Barranquitas in 1826 in the central interior. Known as 'La Ciudad del Río' for the Río de la Plata running through it. Parroquia San José holds records from the 1820s. Pre-1826 records divide between Bayamón and Barranquitas parishes. Coffee and tobacco cultivation dominate hacienda records at AGPR. Hurricane María (2017) devastated this mountain community — some municipal records suffered water damage. The town's isolation in the Cordillera Central meant families intermarried within limited barrios for generations. Check notarial protocols for property inheritance divisions (particiones) that map extended family networks.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Corozal, established 1795, carved from Toa Alta. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Corozal was carved from Toa Alta in 1795 in the north-central interior. Parroquia Niño Jesús de Praga holds records from the late 18th century. As one of the earlier interior settlements, Corozal records document the push into Puerto Rico's mountainous interior. Pre-1795 records are in Toa Alta. Tobacco was the primary crop — cigar manufacturing and tobacco warehouse records provide occupational genealogical data. The municipio's barrios (Abras, Cedro, Cuchillas, Magueyes, Palmarejo, Padilla, Palos Blancos, and others) each have distinctive settlement histories. Check AGPR for Spanish colonial military muster rolls (padrones de milicias) from the late 1700s.

county_metadata.record_losses: US Navy occupation displaced communities and restricted access to parts of Culebra for decades. Some community records were lost during military land expropriations.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Culebra, established 1871, carved from Vieques. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Culebra, an island municipio east of Puerto Rico, was separated from Vieques in 1871. Its small population and island location make genealogical research distinctive. Parroquia Inmaculada Concepción holds limited parish records. The US Navy used Culebra for target practice from 1901-1975 — military records at NARA document evacuations, land acquisitions, and population disruptions. Pre-1871 records are in Vieques. Settlers came from Vieques, the British and US Virgin Islands, and the Puerto Rican mainland. English-speaking Caribbean migrants are noted in civil registry records. Fish trap and boat registration records provide occupational data unique to island communities.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Dorado, established 1842, carved from Toa Baja. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Dorado was carved from Toa Baja in 1842 on the north coast west of San Juan. Known for its beaches and the Laurance Rockefeller resort area. Parroquia Santa Rosa de Lima holds records from the mid-19th century. Pre-1842 records are in Toa Baja. Sugar plantations, particularly along the coast, generated extensive hacienda records and enslaved persons documentation at AGPR. The Hacienda La Sardinera and other estates have well-documented histories. Dorado's proximity to San Juan made it a vacation destination for wealthy families — property records at Registro de la Propiedad trace this development. Check notarial protocols for sugar estate sales and inheritance.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Fajardo, established 1772, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Fajardo, established as a parish around 1772, is the principal east-coast port city. Parroquia Santiago Apóstol holds records from the late 18th century. As parent to Ceiba (1838) and co-parent with Luquillo connections, Fajardo parish records cover the entire eastern tip region. Sugar plantations along the coast and the port economy created diverse records — shipping manifests, customs house records, and merchant ledgers at AGPR. The ferry to Vieques and Culebra islands has operated for over a century. US military presence (Camp Santiago connections, Roosevelt Roads nearby) generated 20th-century records at NARA. Check lighthouse keeper records for Faro de Fajardo.

source_profiles.source_name: Fajardo Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Fajardo / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Fajardo including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Fajardo Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Fajardo district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1772. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Fajardo Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Fajardo. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Fajardo Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Fajardo district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Fajardo district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Fajardo Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Fajardo / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Fajardo. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

source_profiles.source_name: Fajardo Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Fajardo including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Florida, established 1971, carved from Barceloneta, Arecibo, Manatí. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Florida, established in 1971, is Puerto Rico's newest and last-created municipio, carved from Barceloneta, Arecibo, and Manatí. All pre-1971 records are in these three parent municipios' archives. Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Guadalupe has records only from the 1970s. Civil registry records begin in 1971. For genealogical research before 1971, researchers must identify which parent municipio contained their ancestor's barrio: northern barrios from Barceloneta, southern from Arecibo, and eastern from Manatí. The pharmaceutical industry dominates the modern economy. Check Arecibo's parish records extensively, as most of Florida's territory was historically under Arecibo's jurisdiction.

source_profiles.source_name: Florida Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Florida municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1971.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Florida Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Florida / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Florida including births, marriages, and deaths from 1971. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1971. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Florida Deed and Property Records
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad / Municipio de Florida
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property and deed records for Florida municipio from its 1971 creation. Pre-1971 records are in parent municipios Barceloneta, Arecibo, and Manatí. Modern property registration system.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records complete from 1971 formation. Pre-1971 records in parent municipios.

source_profiles.source_name: Florida US Federal Census Records (via parent municipios)
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: US federal census records for the territory now comprising Florida municipio. Pre-1971 census data is found under parent municipios Barceloneta, Arecibo, and Manatí. Puerto Rico was included in US census enumerations from 1910. Decennial census records and American Community Survey data available.
source_profiles.survival_notes: US federal census records for Puerto Rico are generally complete from 1910. Pre-1971 data found under parent municipios.

source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Florida district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Florida district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Florida / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Florida. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Guayama, established 1736, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Guayama, founded as a parish around 1736, is a major south-coast city known as 'La Ciudad Bruja' (City of Witchcraft). Parroquia San Antonio de Padua holds records from the mid-18th century. As parent to Arroyo (1855), Patillas (1811), and co-parent to Salinas, Guayama records cover a broad southeastern coastal region. One of Puerto Rico's largest sugar-producing areas — enslaved persons records, slave registers (registros de esclavos), and coartación (self-purchase) documents at AGPR are extensive. The municipality's large Afro-Puerto Rican population makes church burial and baptism records especially important for Black genealogy. Spanish colonial padrones from the 1700s are at AGPR.

source_profiles.source_name: Guayama Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Guayama municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1736.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Guayama Historical Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Colección Puertorriqueña, UPR / Biblioteca Nacional / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers published in or covering Guayama. Puerto Rico's newspaper tradition began in the early 19th century. Papers include birth/death/marriage announcements, legal notices, estate settlements, and community news. La Gaceta de Puerto Rico (official gazette) published government notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some newspaper runs are incomplete. The Colección Puertorriqueña at UPR Río Piedras has the most extensive holdings. Chronicling America has digitized selected Puerto Rico newspapers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Guayanilla, established 1833, carved from Yauco, Peñuelas. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Guayanilla was carved from Yauco and Peñuelas in 1833 on the south coast. Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Monserrate holds records from the 1830s. Pre-1833 records divide between Yauco and Peñuelas parishes. The petrochemical industry transformed the coastal area in the 20th century. Sugar plantations along the coast generated enslaved persons records. Coffee cultivation in the highland barrios created a different record set — check hacienda records at AGPR. The Bahía de Guayanilla was a shipping point — customs and port records provide genealogical data. Corsican immigration in the 19th century is documented in passenger lists and naturalization records at AGPR.
